county_metadata.parent_county: Original county, Oklahoma Territory (Iowa/Sac & Fox/Potawatomi opening)
county_metadata.record_losses: Courthouse fire 1895 destroyed early territorial records; additional losses from tornado damage 1897
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Lincoln County formed 1891 from the Iowa/Sac & Fox/Potawatomi opening. County seat is Chandler. Named for Abraham Lincoln. Courthouse fire 1895 and tornado 1897 destroyed early records. Home to historically significant All-Black towns (Boley, Langston).
county_metadata.research_tips: Lincoln County was organized in 1891 after the Iowa, Sac and Fox, and Potawatomi lands opening. A courthouse fire in 1895 destroyed early territorial records, and additional losses occurred from tornado damage in 1897, making early research challenging. BLM GLO land patents provide essential backup for destroyed deed records. NARA Fort Worth holds Sac and Fox Agency and Potawatomi Agency records. Lincoln County had several All-Black towns, including the historically significant Boley and Langston (home of Langston University). African American research should include freedmen records and All-Black town community records. County Clerk records in Chandler are incomplete for the early period. The Oklahoma Historical Society holds territorial newspapers. FamilySearch has Oklahoma marriage records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO federal land patents for Lincoln County; County Clerk deed books from 1891

source_profiles.source_name: Lincoln County Land Records (post-loss) / BLM GLO Patents -- County Clerk / BLM
source_profiles.repository: Lincoln County Clerk, Chandler / Bureau of Land Management
source_profiles.coverage_notes: County land records with gaps due to Courthouse fire 1895 destroyed early territorial records; additional losses from tornado damage 1897. BLM GLO federal land patents supplement destroyed county deed books. County Clerk holds surviving and post-loss records
source_profiles.survival_notes: Incomplete: Courthouse fire 1895 destroyed early territorial records; additional losses from tornado damage 1897. BLM GLO patents provide backup for federal land entries

source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, administrations, inventories, guardianships, and estate settlements; District Court has jurisdiction over probate in Oklahoma; records incomplete due to Courthouse fire 1895 destroyed early territorial records; additional losses from tornado damage 1897
source_profiles.survival_notes: Incomplete: Courthouse fire 1895 destroyed early territorial records; additional losses from tornado damage 1897

county_metadata.parent_county: Original county, Oklahoma Territory (1889 Land Run)
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Logan County formed 1890 as original Oklahoma Territory county from the 1889 Land Run. County seat is Guthrie, former territorial capital (1890-1907) and first state capital (1907-1910). Named for John A. Logan. Exceptional territorial-era records.
county_metadata.research_tips: Logan County was one of the original Oklahoma Territory counties formed after the 1889 Land Run. Guthrie, the county seat, served as the territorial capital (1890-1907) and first state capital (1907-1910) before the capital moved to Oklahoma City. This makes Guthrie exceptionally important for territorial-era records. The Oklahoma Territorial Museum in Guthrie has extensive collections. County Clerk records begin from 1889. BLM GLO land patents document original Land Run claims. The Oklahoma Historical Society holds territorial government records, newspapers, and court records from Guthrie. The Scottish Rite Masonic Temple in Guthrie has fraternal records. Logan County had All-Black communities, including Langston. FamilySearch has Oklahoma territorial marriage records. The Logan County Genealogical Society has published local records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO federal land patents for Logan County; County Clerk deed books from 1890

source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival from 1890; GLO patents are complete for federal land entries

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Love County formed 1907 at statehood from the Chickasaw Nation, Indian Territory. County seat is Marietta. Named for Overton Love, a Chickasaw judge. Red River border with Texas.
county_metadata.research_tips: Love County was part of the Chickasaw Nation in Indian Territory. The county borders Texas along the Red River. Chickasaw Nation tribal records (Dawes Rolls, allotment records) at NARA Fort Worth and the Chickasaw Nation headquarters in Ada are essential. County Clerk records in Marietta begin at statehood (1907). The county was predominantly agricultural with cattle ranching. Border families may have records in Texas (Cooke and Grayson counties). The Oklahoma Historical Society holds Indian Territory newspapers and court records. FamilySearch has Oklahoma marriage and probate records. The Love County Heritage Museum has local family materials.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Tribal allotment records (Dawes Act) at NARA Fort Worth for pre-statehood land in Love County; County Clerk deed books from 1907

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Major County formed 1907 at statehood from Woods County. County seat is Fairview. Named for John C. Major. Glass Mountains (mesas) area.
county_metadata.research_tips: Major County was created at statehood (1907) from Woods County and the Cherokee Outlet. For pre-1907 records, check Woods County. County Clerk records in Fairview begin from 1907. BLM GLO land patents document original Cherokee Outlet claims (1893 run). The county was predominantly agricultural with wheat farming and cattle ranching. The Oklahoma Historical Society holds territorial newspapers and census records. The Major County Historical Society has local genealogical materials. FamilySearch has Oklahoma marriage and probate records. Glass Mountains (gloss mountains/mesas) area has local historical significance.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: BLM GLO federal land patents for Major County; County Clerk deed books from 1907

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Marshall County formed 1907 at statehood from the Chickasaw Nation, Indian Territory. County seat is Madill. Named for the Marshall family, prominent Chickasaw citizens.
county_metadata.research_tips: Marshall County was part of the Chickasaw Nation in Indian Territory. Chickasaw Nation tribal records (Dawes Rolls, allotment records, citizenship files) at NARA Fort Worth and the Chickasaw Nation headquarters in Ada are essential. Madill, the county seat, was established at statehood. County Clerk records begin at statehood (1907). Lake Texoma (created 1944) borders the county and some historical communities were relocated. The Oklahoma Historical Society holds Indian Territory newspapers and court records. FamilySearch has Oklahoma marriage and probate records. The Marshall County Genealogical and Historical Society has local materials. Border families may have Texas connections (Grayson County).
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Tribal allotment records (Dawes Act) at NARA Fort Worth for pre-statehood land in Marshall County; County Clerk deed books from 1907

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Mayes County formed 1907 at statehood from the Cherokee Nation, Indian Territory. County seat is Pryor Creek (commonly called Pryor). Named for Samuel Houston Mayes, Cherokee chief.
county_metadata.research_tips: Mayes County was part of the Cherokee Nation in Indian Territory. Cherokee Nation enrollment records, Dawes Rolls, and allotment records are critical for pre-statehood research. The county is named for Samuel Houston Mayes, a Cherokee chief. NARA Fort Worth holds Bureau of Indian Affairs Union Agency records. Grand River Dam Authority and lake construction (Grand Lake, Lake Hudson) in the 1930s-1940s relocated some communities and cemeteries. County Clerk records in Pryor Creek begin at statehood (1907). The Oklahoma Historical Society holds Indian Territory newspapers and court records. FamilySearch has Oklahoma marriage and probate records. The Mayes County Genealogical Society has published cemetery transcriptions. Pryor Creek was the site of a Civil War battle (1862).
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Tribal allotment records (Dawes Act) at NARA Fort Worth for pre-statehood land in Mayes County; County Clerk deed books from 1907

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: McClain County formed 1907 at statehood from Chickasaw Nation and Cleveland County. County seat is Purcell. Named for Charles M. McClain. Important Santa Fe railroad town.
county_metadata.research_tips: McClain County was formed at statehood (1907) from portions of the Chickasaw Nation and Cleveland County. Purcell, the county seat, was a major railroad town on the Santa Fe line. For pre-statehood research, check Chickasaw Nation tribal records (Dawes Rolls, allotment records) at NARA Fort Worth and Cleveland County records. County Clerk records begin at statehood (1907). The Oklahoma Historical Society holds newspapers and court records. The McClain County Historical and Genealogical Society has local genealogical materials. FamilySearch has Oklahoma marriage and probate records. The Purcell Register newspaper archives may be available at OHS.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Tribal allotment records (Dawes Act) at NARA Fort Worth for pre-statehood land in McClain County; County Clerk deed books from 1907

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: McCurtain County formed 1907 at statehood from the Choctaw Nation, Indian Territory. County seat is Idabel. Named for Green McCurtain, Choctaw chief. Far southeastern Oklahoma. Timber industry heritage.
county_metadata.research_tips: McCurtain County was part of the Choctaw Nation in Indian Territory, in the far southeastern corner of Oklahoma. Pre-statehood records center on Choctaw Nation tribal records: Dawes Rolls (Choctaw by Blood, Choctaw Freedmen, Mississippi Choctaw), allotment records, and tribal census records at NARA Fort Worth and the Choctaw Nation headquarters. The county has significant timber industry records. The Beavers Bend area has historical lumber company records. County Clerk records in Idabel begin at statehood (1907). The Oklahoma Historical Society holds Indian Territory newspapers and court records. Freedmen records are important for African American research. The Museum of the Red River in Idabel has regional cultural materials. FamilySearch has Oklahoma marriage and probate records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Tribal allotment records (Dawes Act) at NARA Fort Worth for pre-statehood land in McCurtain County; County Clerk deed books from 1907

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: McIntosh County formed 1907 at statehood from the Creek (Muscogee) Nation, Indian Territory. County seat is Eufaula. Named for the McIntosh family, prominent Creek leaders. All-Black towns and freedmen communities.
county_metadata.research_tips: McIntosh County was part of the Creek (Muscogee) Nation in Indian Territory. Creek Nation enrollment records, Dawes Rolls (Creek by Blood, Creek Freedmen), and allotment records are critical for pre-statehood research. NARA Fort Worth holds Creek Agency records. Eufaula was an important Creek Nation town. The county had several All-Black towns with active freedmen communities. County Clerk records in Eufaula begin at statehood (1907). The Oklahoma Historical Society holds Indian Territory court records and newspapers. Lake Eufaula (created 1964) flooded some historical sites and relocated cemeteries. FamilySearch has Oklahoma marriage and probate records. The McIntosh County Genealogical Society has cemetery transcriptions and family files.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Tribal allotment records (Dawes Act) at NARA Fort Worth for pre-statehood land in McIntosh County; County Clerk deed books from 1907

source_profiles.source_name: McIntosh County Deed Books / Creek (Muscogee) Nation Allotment Records -- County Clerk / NARA
source_profiles.repository: McIntosh County Clerk, Eufaula / NARA Fort Worth
source_profiles.coverage_notes: County deed books for McIntosh County from 1907 (statehood); pre-statehood land was held as Creek (Muscogee) Nation allotments under the Dawes Act -- allotment records at NARA Fort Worth and OHS; County Clerk maintains deeds, mortgages, and liens
source_profiles.survival_notes: County records complete from 1907; allotment records at NARA are substantially complete

source_profiles.source_name: McIntosh County Probate Records -- District Court
source_profiles.repository: McIntosh County District Court, Eufaula
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, administrations, inventories, guardianships, and estate settlements for McIntosh County; District Court has jurisdiction over probate in Oklahoma; guardianship records for minor allottees are particularly important in former Indian Territory counties

source_profiles.source_name: Dawes Rolls -- Creek (Muscogee) Nation Enrollment Records / NARA
source_profiles.repository: NARA Fort Worth / Creek (Muscogee) Nation / Oklahoma Historical Society
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Dawes Commission enrollment records for Creek (Muscogee) citizens (Creek (Muscogee) by Blood, Creek (Muscogee) Freedmen) in McIntosh County area; includes application jackets with family information, census cards, and land allotment details; essential for tracing Creek (Muscogee) families before and after statehood

source_profiles.source_name: McIntosh County Federal and Tribal Census Records
source_profiles.repository: NARA / FamilySearch / Ancestry / Oklahoma Historical Society
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census 1900-1950 for McIntosh County; Creek (Muscogee) Nation census records and Indian Territory schedules at OHS and NARA

source_profiles.source_name: McIntosh County All-Black Towns and Freedmen Records -- OHS / NARA
source_profiles.repository: Oklahoma Historical Society, Oklahoma City / NARA Fort Worth / local community records
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Records of All-Black towns in McIntosh County (Rentiesville, Summit); freedmen enrollment records (Dawes Rolls Freedmen sections); African American community records, school records, church records, and newspaper files at OHS; All-Black town incorporation records at county level

source_profiles.source_name: McIntosh County Marriage Records -- County Clerk / OHS / FamilySearch
source_profiles.repository: McIntosh County Clerk, Eufaula / Oklahoma Historical Society
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage licenses and records for McIntosh County from 1907; County Clerk issued marriage licenses in Oklahoma; OHS holds some county marriage records on microfilm; FamilySearch has indexed Oklahoma marriages; state vital records registration began 1908

source_profiles.source_name: McIntosh County Indian Territory Court Records -- OHS / NARA
source_profiles.repository: Oklahoma Historical Society / NARA Fort Worth
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Indian Territory court records for the McIntosh County area; US courts for Indian Territory (Northern, Central, Southern districts) handled criminal and civil cases; OHS holds court records, case files, and dockets; NARA Fort Worth has federal court records

county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: Murray County formed 1907 at statehood from the Chickasaw Nation, Indian Territory. County seat is Sulphur. Named for William H. Murray ('Alfalfa Bill'). Chickasaw National Recreation Area (formerly Platt National Park).
county_metadata.research_tips: Murray County was part of the Chickasaw Nation in Indian Territory. Sulphur, the county seat, is known for mineral springs that attracted visitors from the territorial era. The Chickasaw National Recreation Area (formerly Platt National Park) is in the county. Chickasaw Nation tribal records (Dawes Rolls, allotment records) at NARA Fort Worth and the Chickasaw Nation headquarters in Ada are essential. County Clerk records begin at statehood (1907). The Oklahoma Historical Society holds Indian Territory newspapers and court records. The Murray County Genealogical Society has local materials. FamilySearch has Oklahoma marriage and probate records. The Davis and Sulphur area newspapers are at OHS.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: Tribal allotment records (Dawes Act) at NARA Fort Worth for pre-statehood land in Murray County; County Clerk deed books from 1907
